bullet_points: Compute auto- and cross-power spectra for all 6 frequency channels, the tSZ ground truth, and the CIB maps using utils.powers with window_alpha=0.5, averaging over a representative subset of 400 patches to reduce sample variance.
Construct the signal covariance matrix S_ell by explicitly including the cross-power spectrum P_ell, tSZ-CIB as a signal component.
Define the noise covariance matrix N_ell using SO and Planck noise realizations, ensuring the SO 90/150 GHz correlation is captured.
Validate the estimator by confirming that the power spectrum of Gaussian white noise matches the theoretical expectation (sigma^2 * pixel_area), and save a validation plot as a PNG.
Save the computed S_ell, N_ell, and all auto/cross power spectra as .npy files to the output directory.
Apply the MWF operator W_ell = (S_ell, obs + N_ell)^-1 S_ell, tSZ-obs (with Tikhonov regularization) to all 1523 signal patches to produce reconstructed y-maps and save these maps to disk.

Develop a multi-dimensional bias-correction lookup table f(Y_obs, sigma_noise, I_CIB) by comparing the reconstructed y-maps against ground-truth tsz.npy, binning by local noise variance and local CIB intensity.
Implement a "constrained" MWF variant that uses the ground-truth tSZ map as an additional input to the covariance model (treating tSZ signal covariance as known exactly).
Compute the transfer function T(ell) and the pixel-level correlation coefficient between the constrained output and the ground truth.
Save the resulting benchmark metrics and a visualization plot of the transfer function as a PNG.
sub_tasks.sub_task: Constrained MWF for Spatial Fidelity Benchmark

bullet_points: Run the detection pipeline on a representative subset of 500 pure noise realizations (SO+Planck noise only) using the same sampling logic for noise indices as the signal patches.
Calculate the FDR as a function of SNR to establish a statistically rigorous detection threshold that accounts for the "look-elsewhere effect" across the 1523 patches.
Perform peak-finding on the standard (unconstrained) MWF output map using the threshold determined in Step 4.
Apply a spatial matched filter (beam-convolved Arnaud/GNFW profile) in Fourier space at a representative angular scale (e.g., theta_500 approx 1–5 arcmin).
Cross-match detected peaks against the ground-truth catalog within a 1.5 arcmin radius to assign true-positive/false-positive labels.
Quantify detection completeness and purity as a function of halo mass, local CIB intensity, and local noise variance.
Apply the bias-correction lookup table to detected candidates to recover integrated Compton-Y within a 5 arcmin aperture.
Compare recovered integrated-Y against ground-truth integrated-Y (from tsz.npy) and measure the mass bias.
Define the mass proxy as the peak Compton-y value from the ground-truth map.
Generate a single multi-panel figure combining the transfer function T(ell), completeness/purity curves, and mass bias distribution.
Ensure all plots are saved as high-resolution PNGs with descriptive filenames.
Compile results, explicitly referencing all saved plot filenames (e.g., transfer function, completeness/purity curves, mass bias histogram).
Include quantitative results such as the 90% completeness mass threshold, purity at the chosen SNR cut, and mean fractional mass bias, drawn from the engineer's outputs.
Interpret the impact of including P_ell, tSZ-CIB on signal separation and the effectiveness of the bias-correction table.
